title: Constraints Determine Output

content.statement: The weakest link caps the system.
content.question: What's the one constraint you've been optimizing around instead of actually fixing?
content.equation: Max Output = Capacity of the Weakest Constraint
content.mental_model: A chain breaks at its weakest link.
content.business_lens: Fix the bottleneck, not the strongest part.
content.life_lens: Energy, time, or clarity, whichever is lowest sets your day.
sop: Map your process end to end and note the capacity, speed, volume, or quality output, of each stage.
Rate the capacity of each stage 1-10 to find the lowest score, that's your real constraint (see calculation, page 5).
Ignore every other stage until the constraint is measurably widened.
Add capacity, remove a step, or delegate at that single point.
Re-map the chain afterward, the constraint has likely moved somewhere else. Repeat.
questions: What's the one constraint you've been optimizing around instead of actually fixing?
If you doubled the capacity of your weakest link tomorrow, what would break next, and have you already met that constraint?
Which part of your process gets the least attention precisely because everyone knows it's the problem?

definitions.def: Every system has a single constraint that caps output.

content.equation_example: Rate the capacity of each part of your system 1-10: Marketing 9, Sales 8, Fulfillment 3, Support 7. Max Output = the lowest score, 3, not the average. Higher means your bottleneck itself is strong and the whole system can run near capacity; lower means one weak link is capping everything else, no matter how good the rest is.
content.closing_statement: Fix the weakest link, not the strongest one.

caption:
Principle Nº 105: Constraints Determine Output

The weakest link caps the system.

Ask yourself: What's the one constraint you've been optimizing around instead of actually fixing?
